author: Plato
structure: Five definitions, each refuted, terminating in aporia

turns.speaker: Euthyphro
turns.content: Piety is what I am doing now — prosecuting wrongdoers, even one's own father, as Zeus punished Kronos.

added: Piety can be defined by example (prosecuting wrongdoers)

euthyphro: Piety can be defined by example (prosecuting wrongdoers)

turns.speaker: Socrates
turns.content: I did not ask for an example of piety but for the form (eidos) itself — the single characteristic by which all pious things are pious. An example is not a definition.

added: A definition must state the form (eidos), not enumerate instances

euthyphro: Piety can be defined by example (prosecuting wrongdoers)
socrates: A definition must state the form (eidos), not enumerate instances

turns.speaker: Euthyphro
turns.content: Piety is what is dear to the gods (to theophiles). Impiety is what is hateful to the gods.

added: Piety = what is dear to the gods
Impiety = what is hateful to the gods
removed: Piety can be defined by example (prosecuting wrongdoers)

euthyphro: Piety = what is dear to the gods
Impiety = what is hateful to the gods
socrates: A definition must state the form (eidos), not enumerate instances

turns.speaker: Socrates
turns.content: You yourself agree the gods quarrel and disagree. If they disagree about what is just, then the same act may be dear to some gods and hateful to others. By your definition, the same act would be both pious and impious — a contradiction.

added: The gods disagree about justice
If gods disagree, the same act is both dear and hateful to the gods
Definition 2 produces contradiction: same act is pious and impious

euthyphro: Piety = what is dear to the gods
Impiety = what is hateful to the gods
The gods disagree about justice
socrates: A definition must state the form (eidos), not enumerate instances
If gods disagree, the same act is both dear and hateful to the gods
Definition 2 produces contradiction: same act is pious and impious

turns.speaker: Euthyphro
turns.content: Piety is what all the gods love. Impiety is what all the gods hate. (Socrates proposes the 'all gods' amendment to resolve the disagreement problem; Euthyphro adopts it.)

added: Piety = what ALL the gods love
Impiety = what ALL the gods hate
removed: Piety = what is dear to the gods
Impiety = what is hateful to the gods

euthyphro: Piety = what ALL the gods love
Impiety = what ALL the gods hate
The gods disagree about justice
socrates: A definition must state the form (eidos), not enumerate instances
If gods disagree, the same act is both dear and hateful to the gods
Definition 2 produces contradiction: same act is pious and impious

turns.speaker: Socrates
turns.content: Is the pious loved by the gods because it is pious, or is it pious because it is loved by the gods? These are different. 'Being loved' is a passive state caused by the gods' attitude. 'Being pious' is a property of the act itself. God-lovedness is an effect of piety, not its essence. Your definition gives an attribute (pathos) of piety, not its nature (ousia).

added: Being-loved-by-gods is a passive state (pathos), not an essence (ousia)
If piety causes god-love, then god-love cannot define piety (circular)
Definition 3 conflates attribute with essence

euthyphro: Piety = what ALL the gods love
Impiety = what ALL the gods hate
The gods disagree about justice
The pious is loved because it is pious (not pious because loved)
socrates: A definition must state the form (eidos), not enumerate instances
Being-loved-by-gods is a passive state (pathos), not an essence (ousia)
If piety causes god-love, then god-love cannot define piety (circular)
Definition 3 conflates attribute with essence

turns.speaker: Euthyphro
turns.content: Piety is that part of justice concerned with the care (therapeia) of the gods, as there are other parts of justice concerned with the care of humans.

added: Piety is a species of justice
Piety = care (therapeia) of the gods
removed: Piety = what ALL the gods love

euthyphro: Piety is a species of justice
Piety = care (therapeia) of the gods
The gods disagree about justice
The pious is loved because it is pious (not pious because loved)
socrates: A definition must state the form (eidos), not enumerate instances
Being-loved-by-gods is a passive state (pathos), not an essence (ousia)
If piety causes god-love, then god-love cannot define piety (circular)
Definition 3 conflates attribute with essence

turns.speaker: Socrates
turns.content: Care (therapeia) of horses makes horses better. Care of dogs makes dogs better. Does your care of the gods make the gods better? Surely not — the gods are already perfect. So 'care' cannot mean what it normally means. What kind of service (hyperetike) to the gods is piety?

added: Care (therapeia) normally improves its object
Gods cannot be improved (they are perfect)
Piety-as-care does not improve the gods
Piety must be service (hyperetike), but to what end?

euthyphro: Piety is a species of justice
Piety = care (therapeia) of the gods
Gods cannot be improved (they are perfect)
The pious is loved because it is pious (not pious because loved)
socrates: A definition must state the form (eidos), not enumerate instances
Care (therapeia) normally improves its object
Piety-as-care does not improve the gods
Piety must be service (hyperetike), but to what end?

turns.refutation_target: Definition 4 — 'care' is inapplicable to perfect beings

turns.speaker: Euthyphro
turns.content: Piety is knowledge of how to sacrifice and pray — giving to the gods and asking from them.

added: Piety = knowledge of sacrifice (giving) and prayer (asking)

euthyphro: Piety is a species of justice
Piety = knowledge of sacrifice (giving) and prayer (asking)
Gods cannot be improved (they are perfect)
The pious is loved because it is pious (not pious because loved)
socrates: A definition must state the form (eidos), not enumerate instances
Care (therapeia) normally improves its object
Piety-as-care does not improve the gods
Piety must be service (hyperetike), but to what end?

turns.speaker: Socrates
turns.content: Giving to the gods and asking from them — this is a kind of commerce (emporike) between gods and humans. But what do the gods gain from our gifts? If piety is giving what pleases the gods, then piety is what is pleasing to the gods — and we are back to 'what the gods love.' We have gone in a circle.

added: Sacrifice-and-prayer reduces to giving what pleases the gods
What pleases the gods = what the gods love
Definition 5 collapses into Definition 3
APORIA: the argument has returned to its starting point

euthyphro: Piety is a species of justice
Piety = knowledge of sacrifice (giving) and prayer (asking)
Gods cannot be improved (they are perfect)
The pious is loved because it is pious (not pious because loved)
socrates: A definition must state the form (eidos), not enumerate instances
Sacrifice-and-prayer reduces to giving what pleases the gods
What pleases the gods = what the gods love
Definition 5 collapses into Definition 3
APORIA: the argument has returned to its starting point

terminal_state.description: The fifth definition reduces to the third, which was already refuted. The commitment store is circular — no consistent definition of piety survives. Euthyphro departs without resolution.
